channel.title: Stories by Linuka Arambawela on Medium
channel.description: Stories by Linuka Arambawela on Medium

image.title: Stories by Linuka Arambawela on Medium

item.title: The 30-Second Secret: How Your Phone Generates Unbreakable Codes
item.dc:creator: Linuka Arambawela
item.content:encoded:
TOTP stands for Time based One Time Password. It is the technology behind apps like Google Authenticator and Microsoft Authenticator.

Think of it as a secret handshake that changes every 30 seconds.

The Secret Ingredients

For TOTP to work, your device (phone) and the server (e.g. Google or Instagram) must agree on two things:

  1. The Shared Secret: This is a unique string of random characters. When you scan a QR code during setup, you are actually transferring this secret key to your phone.
  2. The Current Time: Both devices use the current Unix time (the number of seconds since January 1, 1970).

How the Code is Generated

The process follows a specific mathematical recipe called the HMAC based One Time Password (HOTP) algorithm, but with a time twist.

1. Timesteps

Instead of using the exact second (which would be too fast), the algorithm divides the current time into “steps” usually 30 seconds.

T = [(Current Time — T₀)/X]
  • T is the time factor.
  • T₀ is the start time (usually 0).
  • X is the step (30 seconds).

2. Hashing

The secret key and the time factor are mixed together using a cryptographic hashing function (usually SHA-1).

Hash= HMAC — SHA1(Secret Key, T)

This creates a long, complex string of data that is impossible to reverse engineer.

3. Truncation

The long hash is “shaved down” to a human readable format usually a 6 digit number. This is the code you see on your screen.

Why is it secure?

  • No Transmission: The code is never sent from the server to your phone. Both sides calculate it independently. Even if someone intercepts your 6 digit code, it becomes useless 30 seconds later.
  • Offline Capability: Your phone doesn’t need internet to generate the code because it only needs the Secret Key (stored locally) and the current time.
  • Brute Force Resistance: Trying to guess a 6 digit code in 30 seconds is statistically very difficult, and most services lock you out after a few wrong attempts.
“My code isn’t working!”
The algorithm uses only time, so if the internal time on your phone is off by one minute, the math will not match the server’s math. Most sync problems can be solved by setting the time on your phone to Automatic.

item.title: ClawdBot Full Tutorial: How to Set Up Your Local AI Powerhouse
item.dc:creator: Linuka Arambawela
item.content:encoded:
In the world of AI, there are tools that answer questions, and then there are tools that get things done. ClawdBot, now officially known as OpenClaw, is the latter. Unlike other tools like ChatGPT or Claude, which run right in your browser and essentially go away when you close your browser tab, OpenClaw is a local AI assistant that runs right on your computer.

It doesn’t just chat; it makes files, writes code, surfs the web, and works with the apps you use every day, like Telegram, Slack, Google, and Discord.

This guide will take you through everything you need to know to get your own local AI assistant up and running in under 15 minutes.

What is ClawdBot (OpenClaw)?

At its core, OpenClaw is a local gateway for AI. It acts as the bridge between powerful Large Language Models (LLMs) like Claude 3.5 Sonnet or GPT-4o and your actual computer.

Key Features:

  • Self-Hosted: Runs on your hardware (Mac, Windows, or Linux).
  • Persistent Memory: It remembers your preferences and past conversations across sessions.
  • Full System Access: It can run bash commands, manage files, and automate tasks.
  • Multi-Platform: Chat with it via Telegram, WhatsApp, Discord, or Slack.
  • Privacy-First: Your context and files stay on your machine, not on a third-party server.

Step 1: Getting Your API Keys

Before installing the software, you need a “brain” for it. OpenClaw is the body; the LLM is the mind.

  1. Choose a Provider: Most users prefer Anthropic (Claude) for its coding abilities, but OpenAI or OpenRouter (which offers free models) work too.
  2. Generate the Key:
  • Go to console.anthropic.com or platform.openai.com.
  • Navigate to the API Keys section.
  • Create a new key, name it “OpenClaw,” and copy it somewhere safe. You will need this during the setup wizard.

Step 2: Installation

OpenClaw is designed to be installed with a single command.

  1. Open your Terminal (Command Prompt/PowerShell on Windows, Terminal on Mac/Linux).
  2. Run the Installer: Paste the following command and hit Enter:
curl -fsSL https://openclaw.ai/install.sh | bash
  1. (Note: If you don’t have Node.js or Git installed, the script will usually prompt you to install them or do it for you.)

Step 3: The Onboarding Wizard

Once the installation finishes, the configuration wizard will start automatically.

  • Acknowledge Risks: It will ask if you understand that this tool is powerful and can execute commands. Select yes.
  • Choose Mode: Select “Quick Start” for the easiest experience.
  • Select Provider: Choose the provider you got a key for (e.g., Anthropic).
  • Paste API Key: Paste the key you saved in Step 1.
  • Choose a Model: For a balance of speed and intelligence, Claude 3.5 Sonnet (or Sonnet 4.0 if available) is highly recommended.

Step 4: Connecting to Telegram

To turn OpenClaw into a mobile assistant, you’ll want to link it to Telegram.

  1. Find @BotFather: In Telegram, search for @BotFather and send /newbot.
  2. Get Your Token: Follow the prompts to name your bot. BotFather will give you an API Token.
  3. Enter Token in Terminal: When the OpenClaw wizard asks for your messaging channel, select Telegram and paste that token.
  4. Pair Your Account: * Open your new bot in Telegram and send /start.
  • The bot will give you a Pairing Code.
  • Back in your terminal, run the command provided (usually openclaw pairing approve telegram [CODE]).

Step 5: Using the Web Dashboard

If you prefer a visual interface over the terminal, OpenClaw runs a local web server.

  • URL: http://localhost:18789
  • What you can do: Monitor logs, change settings, install new “Skills” (like Google Drive or Web Search integration), and view your full chat history.

Important: Security and Costs

Because OpenClaw can run commands on your PC, you should be careful.

  • Sandboxing: If you’re tech-savvy, consider running it in a Docker container to keep it isolated from your sensitive files.
  • API Costs: You are paying the AI provider per message. Using “heavy” models like Claude 3 Opus can get expensive quickly. Start with Sonnet or GPT-4o-mini to keep costs low.

Read more: https://openclaw.ai/

item.title: The Unexpected Beginning
item.dc:creator: Linuka Arambawela
item.content:encoded:
I never thought my first real work experience would come while I was still in school. When I applied to join Richmond Live, it became my first actual interview for a position something I had never faced before. Getting selected felt unreal.

As a school boy, I was suddenly exposed to real tech, real responsibilities, and real pressure in a very short time. That experience shaped me more than I realized back then. Today, wherever I am and whatever I do, a big part of it is because of the exposure I gained through Richmond Live.

Now, let’s talk about what Richmond Live really is and why it became the unexpected beginning of my journey

What is Richmond Live?

Richmond Live is more than a school media team. It works like a small company. The crew is around 80 members, operating across multiple departments, including DigiOps, camera crew, editorial, graphic design, video editing, photography, social media, HR, finance, and even commentators. Each unit handles real responsibilities, just like a professional production house.

For a school student, it was an incredible opportunity to learn so many skills at such a young age. Seniors who were already working in the industry shared their knowledge with us, but what made Richmond Live truly special was the culture, there was no seniority. Every idea mattered, no matter who it came from.

We managed budgets over 1 million LKR for the big match productions and executed everything according to a clear and well planned structure. And it wasn’t just the Lovers’ Quarrel we covered many other major school events with the same level of professionalism.

Richmond Live isn’t just a team. It was a real world learning environment disguised inside a school.

My experience

My journey began with an interview. The questions were friendly, practical, and focused on real situations rather than theory, which immediately made me feel comfortable. That was my first exposure to a real interview environment.

I initially joined the production team, where I learned how professional production works from planning to execution. Along the way, I didn’t just stick to one role. I explored videography, DigiOps, and other technical areas, gaining hands on experience across multiple domains.

Every senior member was incredibly supportive. Since many of them were already working in the industry, we were exposed to the latest technology, tools, and workflows used in real production environments. That made learning faster and more practical.

Richmond Live also operates two websites https://richmondlive.lk/ , http://loversquarrel.lk/ and a mobile application, available on both the Apple app store and Google play store, which gave us experience working on real digital platforms used by thousands of people.

Today, I’m part of the DigiOps team as a senior member, a role that reflects how much I’ve grown since that first interview. Looking back, the amount I learned in such a short time is something I’m truly grateful for.

Richmond Live was more than a school experience for me, it was the foundation that shaped my mindset, skills, and professionalism. What began as a simple application turned into an opportunity that exposed me to real world production, teamwork, and responsibility at a very young age.

If you’d like to explore the work we do at Richmond Live, you can find us on the platforms below:

🌐 Official Website: https://richmondlive.lk/

📱 Mobile App: https://onelink.to/g7vbgs

▶️ YouTube: https://www.youtube.com/@RichmondLive-RL

📘 Facebook: https://www.facebook.com/share/182cCknGzY/

item.title: The Magic Behind “Sign in with Google” — How WSO2 Identity Server Makes Federated Authentication…
item.dc:creator: Linuka Arambawela
item.content:encoded:
The Magic Behind “Sign in with Google” How WSO2 Identity Server Makes Federated Authentication Look Easy

Have you ever clicked one of those “Sign in with Google” buttons? Let us take you on a journey exploring what they do behind the scenes in WSO2 Identity Server and Asgardeo.

What is the fuss about federated authentication?

Imagine you’re building the next big social media app. Your users don’t want to create another username and password — they’re tired of managing dozens of accounts! Enter federated authentication — the superhero of the identity world.

With WSO2 Identity Server (IS) / Asgardeo, you can let users login with their existing Google, Facebook, Microsoft, or any other identity provider(IdP) accounts. It’s like having a universal key that opens all doors!

🎭 The Cast of Characters
Before we dive into the action, let’s meet our main players:

👤 User: Someone trying to access your application
🏢 Application: Your business application
🛡️ WSO2 Identity Server: The intelligent middleman orchestrating everything
🌐 Google OAuth2: The external identity provider(Fedarated IdP)

🎬 Act 1: The Journey Begins
Let’s stick with Bob, for example, a typical user who is trying to get into the HR portal of his company who forgets passwords all the time. Good news, the portal has a “Login with Google” functionality!

🎯 Scene 1–4: The Initial Approach
Bob tries to log into the HR portal but is sent to WSO2 IS instead. WSO2 IS is like a smart bouncer that checks what authentication options are available. In Bob’s case, WSO2 IS presents him various identity providers, including Google, for authentication.

🎯 Scene 5–7: Making the Choice
Bob chooses to log in with Google, and off to work WSO2 IS goes. The system:

  • Activates the GoogleOAuth2Authenticator (our Google specialist
  • Builds a special OAuth2 URL with all the right parameters
  • Redirects Bob to Google’s login page

This is where the magic URL is born:

https://accounts.google.com/o/oauth2/auth
?client_id=your-app-id
&redirect_uri=https://your-wso2-server.com/commonauth
&scope=openid email profile
&response_type=code
&state=super-secret-session-id

🎬 Act 2: The Google Adventure

🎯 Scene 8–11: The Authentication Dance
As always, Bob logs into Google using its blue-and-white interface. Validating his identity using Google’s systems, Bob does not realize that instead of logging him in, Google redirects him BACK to WSO2 IS with a special token to validate his identity.

In the context of his systems, think of this token as a form of a “golden ticket” that Bob has received in the form of trust from Google but has not fully claimed the prize yet.

🎬 Act 3: The Behind-the-Scenes Magic
To him, as with Bob, a simple redirect does not convey the great deal of magic that is taking place behind the scenes. The real work is being done the harmony of systems working together:

🎯 Scene 12–16: The Secret Exchange

  1. Extract Authorization Code: WSO2 IS grabs that golden ticket from the URL
  2. Token Exchange: Like a secret agent, it exchanges the code for an access token by making a backend call to Google
  3. User Info Retrieval: Using the access token, it asks Google: “Hey, tell me about Bob!”
  4. Claims Processing: Google responds with Bob’s information (name, email, profile picture, etc.)

🎯 Scene 17: The Transformation Magic

Now comes the really cool part! WSO2 IS / Asgardeo acts like a universal translator:

  • Claim Mapping: Converts Google’s way of saying “email address” to your application’s way.
  • User Provisioning: Decides whether to create a new user account or update an existing one.
  • Session Creation: Creates a secure session so Bob doesn’t have to login again anytime soon.

🎬 Act 4: The Grand Finale

🎯 Scene 18–20:

WSO2 IS now needs to speak your application’s language. Whether your app expects:

  • SAML tokens (like a fancy XML certificate)
  • OIDC tokens (modern JSON-based tokens)
  • OAuth2 tokens (for API access)

WSO2 IS generates the right format and sends Bob back to the HR portal with his digital passport. The application validates the token and Bob is logged in!

Why is This So Important?

For Users:

  • Elimination of password fatigue.
  • Trust-based one-click login.
  • Uniform experience throughout all applications.

For Developers:

  • Skip the tedious work of building authentication from zero.
  • Support for popular protocols like SAML, OIDC, OAuth2.
  • Static security and compliance makes things easier.

For IT Administrators:

  • Centralized identity management
  • Advanced security features (MFA, risk-base authentication)
  • Detailed audit logs and analytics

Ready to Get Started?

This federation process is just the beginning! WSO2 Identity Server and Asgardeo can connect with:

  • Social Providers: Google, Facebook, Twitter, LinkedIn
  • Enterprise Providers: Active Directory, LDAP, Microsoft Azure AD
  • Custom Providers: Your own OAuth2 or SAML identity systems
  • The next time you see a “Login with $provider” button, remember the incredible journey happening behind the scenes. WSO2 Identity Server is orchestrating a complex dance of redirects, token exchanges, and claim mappings to make your life easier!

Looking to explore more? The WSO2 Identity Server documentation along with the community offer great avenues to identity federation.. Welcome to the exciting world of modern identity management!

Identity Server Docs: https://is.docs.wso2.com/en/latest/
Asgardeo Docs: https://wso2.com/asgardeo/docs/

item.title: OIDC vs SAML
item.dc:creator: Linuka Arambawela
item.content:encoded:
As developers choosing the right authentication protocol is major decision when designing secure and scalable applications. Two of the most widely adopted standards are OpenID Connect (OIDC) and Security assertion Markup Language(SAML). Both of them enable Single sign-On (SSO) and federated identity, but the differ in design, usage and in many more aspects.

Let’s see a quick comparison of these two.

How Each Protocol Works

SAML

  • Design: SAML is the base of authentication in the majority of big organizations. It gives users access to multiple in-house applications with single login(SSO).
  • Flow: The Service Provider re-directs the user to the Identity Provider (IdP), which verifies the user and sends an XML based assertion that contains the user’s identity and attributes
  • Security: Because SAML assertions are digitally signed and typically encrypted, they provide effective security for regulated environments.
  • Complexity: Integration requires working with XML, digital signatures, and typically there are more configurations between IdP and SP.

OIDC (OpenID Connect)

  • Design: OIDC is an identity layer which is built on top of OAuth 2.0, using RESTful APIs and JSON, making it ideal for cloud-native, mobile, and API-centric applications.
  • Flow: The client requests authentication from the OpenID Provider. After a successful login, the provider issues an ID token (JWT) and optionally access or refresh tokens.
  • Developer Experience: Easier to implement because of JSON and REST, with strong support in modern web frameworks and cloud platforms.
  • Flexibility: OIDC is highly interoperable and scales well with distributed and microservices architectures.

When to Use SAML

  • Legacy or enterprise web applications: SAML is the de facto standard for SSO in large organizations, especially where existing infrastructure and compliance requirements call for it..
  • Regulated industries: The mature and advanced security model of SAML is utilized in finance, healthcare, and government.
  • Identity federation: SAML is well suited to providing SSO between partners or across large enterprise portals.

When to Use OIDC

  • Modern web and mobile apps: OIDC’s lightweight JWTs and RESTful APIs enable Single page apps, mobile clients, and cloud-native apps as a natural fit.
  • API and microservices security: OIDC complements OAuth 2.0 naturally and is a token-based API access control.
  • Third-party/social login: OIDC is the protocol in most social logins (Google, Microsoft, GitHub), making federating consumer app identity easy.

Key Developer Considerations

  • Integration Complexity: OIDC is generally easier and faster to implement. SAML requires more XML, digital signature expertise, and more configuration.
  • Performance: OIDC JWTs are smaller and faster to process with lower system overhead compared to SAML’s XML assertions.
  • Security: Both protocols are secure when executed correctly. SAML’s longer history makes it a safer choice in legacy and compliance-driven infrastructures, but OIDC security is solid and actively evolving.

Can You Use Both?

Yes. Most use SAML for enterprise SSO (on-prem apps) and OIDC for cloud, mobile, API scenarios. They’re not incompatible and can exist in a hybrid identity architecture.

Conclusion for Developers:

  • Choose SAML for legacy, enterprise, and regulated web apps where XML-based SSO is required.
  • Choose OIDC for modern, scalable, API-driven, and mobile-friendly applications where developer agility and matter most.

Understanding these differences will help you select the proper protocol for your stack to offer secure, transparent authentication to your users.

item.title: Streamline Your Meeting Management with Ballerina’s Zoom Scheduler Connector
item.dc:creator: Linuka Arambawela
item.content:encoded:
This article was written using Ballerina SwanLake Update 12 (2201.12.0)

In today’s hybrid work landscape, managing meetings across time zones and packed calendars can be a challenge. As part of my internship onboarding project at WSO2, I created the Ballerina Zoom Scheduler connector to simplify meeting management by integrating Zoom’s powerful scheduling features directly into Ballerina applications. This tool makes it easier to automate, coordinate, and analyze schedules with minimal code.

Why Use It?,

  • Type-safe API calls with compile-time validation
  • Built-in OAuth 2.0 with auto token refresh
  • Error-friendly architecture using Ballerina’s error model
  • Perfect for microservices and integrations

Prerequisites:

  1. Setup App in Zoom Marketplace
  2. Download and install Ballerina
  3. Install VS Code + Ballerina extension

Getting Started: Your First Schedule

After setting up your Zoom app (Read here), you can create a meeting like this:

1. Setup your Config.toml

clientId = "Your_Client_ID"
clientSecret = "Your_Client_Secret"
refreshToken = "Your_Refresh_Token"
userId = "Zoom_User_ID"

2. Import Dependencies

import ballerina/io;
import ballerinax/zoom.scheduler as zoom;

3. Initialize the Zoom Client

configurable string clientId = ?;
configurable string clientSecret = ?;
configurable string refreshToken = ?;
configurable string userId = ?;

final zoom:Client zoomClient = check new ({
    auth: {
        clientId,
        clientSecret,
        refreshUrl: "https://zoom.us/oauth/token",
        refreshToken
    }
});

Fetch User Info

Let’s fetch basic Zoom user info to personalize the interaction.

zoom:InlineResponse2007 userInfo = check zoomClient->/users/[userId].get();
io:println("Meeting Scheduler for ", userInfo.displayName ?: "User");

Define a Smart Meeting Schedule

Using the ballerina/time module, we can build well-structured time values and recurring availability slots:

zoom:ScheduleravailabilitySegmentsRecurrenceSun[] weekdaySchedule = [{'start: "09:00", end: "17:00"}];
zoom:ScheduleravailabilitySegmentsRecurrenceSun[] weekendSchedule = [{'start: "09:00", end: "12:00"}];

zoom:SchedulerSchedulesBody newSchedule = {
    addOnType: "zoomMeeting",
    summary: "Engineering Standup",
    description: "Daily team sync meeting",
    duration: 30,
    location: "Virtual - Zoom",
    timeZone: "Asia/Shanghai",
    
availabilityRules: [{
        segments: [{
            'start: "2025-06-27T01:00:00Z",
             end: "2025-06-27T02:00:00Z"
        }],
        segmentsRecurrence: {
            sun: weekendSchedule,
                mon: weekdaySchedule,
                tue: weekdaySchedule,
                wed: weekdaySchedule,
                thu: weekdaySchedule,
                fri: weekdaySchedule,
                sat: weekendSchedule
        },
        timeZone: "Asia/Shanghai"
    }],
    buffer: {
            before: 15,
            after: 15
        },
        customFields: [{
            name: "Meeting Agenda",
            format: "text",
            required: true,
            position: 0
        }]
    };

Create the Schedule

zoom:InlineResponse2011 createdSchedule = check zoomClient->/schedules.post(
    payload = newSchedule
);
io:println("Created Schedule: ", createdSchedule.summary);

List Existing Schedules

zoom:InlineResponse2005|error allSchedules = zoomClient->/schedules.get(
    userId = userId,
    pageSize = 10
);

if allSchedules is zoom:InlineResponse2005 {
    if allSchedules.items is zoom:InlineResponse2005Items[] {
        io:println("Total Schedules: ", allSchedules.items.length());
    }
}

Advanced Features

  • Custom fields for extra input (e.g., notes, preferences)
  • Buffer management between meetings
  • Multi-time zone support with recurrence rules

Best Practices

  • Use configurable for sensitive configs
  • Handle errors gracefully using Ballerina’s type system
  • Watch out for Zoom API rate limits and add retries if needed

The Ballerina Zoom Scheduler connector is ready to help you build intelligent scheduling platforms — whether for teams, customers, or classrooms.

  • Explore the connector: Ballerina Central
  • Check full examples: Meeting Scheduler, Availability Manager
  • GitHub Repo: github.com/ballerina-platform

With just a few lines of Ballerina, you can automate availability, track usage, and build smart integrations saving hours of manual work.

Streamline Your Meeting Management with Ballerina’s Zoom Scheduler Connector was originally published in Ballerina Swan Lake Tech Blog on Medium, where people are continuing the conversation by highlighting and responding to this story.

item.title: New to open source? Learn, Contribute, and Grow
item.dc:creator: Linuka Arambawela
item.content:encoded:
What is Open Source?

Open source means software where the code is publicly available. Anyone can view, modify, or share it for free! Think of it like a recipe everyone can tweak and improve together. Popular examples include Linux, WordPress, and Firefox.

Why Open Source Rocks

Here’s why developers and companies love it:

  1. Free to use: No license fees.
  2. Flexibility: Customize software to fit your needs.
  3. Community power: Thousands of developers test and fix bugs, making projects reliable.
  4. Career boost: Build real-world skills and connect with mentors.
  5. Innovation: Diverse perspectives lead to creative solutions

Here’s some words from CEO of WSO2 Mr.Sanjiva about Open Source

Open Source Programs to Know

🎓 Google Summer of Code (GSoC)

What? A 3-month program where you earn stipends ($$$) for contributing to open source projects .

How it works?

  • Register on GSoC’s website.
  • Organizations list project ideas (e.g., robotics, AI, HealthCare).
  • You submit proposals, get paired with mentors, and code over the summer.
  • Eligibility: Open to anyone 18+ (students and professionals).
  • Pro tip: Start contributing to projects early — fixing small bugs or docs helps you stand out.

🎃 Hacktoberfest

What?: A month-long October event celebrating open source. Complete 4 pull requests (code/documentation fixes) to earn rewards.

How to join?

  • Register on Hacktoberfest’s website.
  • Look for projects tagged hacktoberfest on GitHub/GitLab.
  • Rewards: Digital badges, swag, or even trees planted in your name.

Here are some badges I recived from Hacktoberfest

Getting Started: Your Step-by-Step Roadmap

1️⃣ Pick a Project You Care About

  • Find a project that aligns with your skills: Check this GitHub repo.
  • Or explore by interest: GitHub’s Explore page suggests projects based on your skills.
  • Beginner-friendly tags: Look for good-first-issue labels.

💡 Example: If you’re into web development, try contributing to freeCodeCamp’s docs.

2️⃣ Learn the Rules of the Project

Every project has guidelines. Read these files:

  • README.md: The project’s “user manual.”
  • CONTRIBUTING.md: How to submit fixes (e.g., coding style).
  • CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md: Community behavior expectations.

💡 Pro tip: Join the project’s Slack/Discord to ask questions!

3️⃣ Contribute Without Coding

You don’t need to be a coding expert to help:

  • Fix typos in documentation.
  • Report bugs (e.g., “The login button doesn’t work on mobile”).
  • Translate guides into other languages.
  • Design logos or improve UI/UX.

💡 Fun fact: Good docs can boost productivity by 50% (source)!

4️⃣ Make Your First Pull Request (PR)

  1. Fork the project: Create your copy of the code.
  2. Make changes: Fix a bugs, add features or tackle a good-first-issue.
  3. Submit a PR: Describe your changes clearly.
  4. Celebrate: Maintainers will review your work!

Why Your Contribution Matters

  • You’ll learn faster: Real projects > tutorials.
  • Build a portfolio: Employers love open source experience.
  • Join a global community: Meet mentors, friends, and collaborators from all around the world.

Ready to start? The open source world can’t wait to see what you’ll create! Need inspiration?

  • Watch: Full beginner guide

Happy coding! 💻

Follow me:

GitHub: https://github.com/LinukaAr

Web: linukaarambawela.live

item.title: Cloud Computing 101: Introduction to Azure
item.dc:creator: Linuka Arambawela
item.content:encoded:
What is Cloud Computing?

Cloud computing is a revolutionary approach to accessing and managing IT resources, offering computing services such as servers, storage, databases, networking, and software over the internet. By leveraging the cloud, businesses can innovate faster, utilize flexible resources, and achieve significant cost savings. Instead of owning and maintaining physical infrastructure, you pay only for the services you use, enabling more efficient operations and scalability as your business grows.

Benefits of Cloud Computing

1. Cost Efficiency

Cloud computing eliminates the need for significant upfront investments in hardware and software. By moving to the cloud, businesses can avoid the capital expense of setting up and maintaining on-premises data centers, resulting in optimized IT costs.

2. Speed and Agility

Cloud services are typically available on-demand, allowing businesses to provision vast amounts of resources in minutes. This flexibility reduces the pressure of capacity planning and enables quicker adaptation to changing business needs.

3. Global Scale

The cloud allows you to scale resources elastically, delivering the right amount of IT resources when and where they are needed. This global reach ensures optimal performance and availability, regardless of your location.

4. Enhanced Productivity

Cloud computing minimizes the time spent on managing IT infrastructure. By automating many tasks, such as hardware setup and software updates, IT teams can focus on more strategic initiatives

5. Improved Performance

Cloud providers operate a global network of data centers equipped with the latest hardware, ensuring fast and reliable service. This setup reduces network latency and benefits from economies of scale.

6. Reliability and Security

Cloud computing offers robust solutions for data backup, disaster recovery, and business continuity. With data mirrored across multiple sites, the cloud ensures higher reliability. Leading cloud providers also implement strict security measures to protect your data and infrastructure from potential threats.

Types of Cloud Computing

Public Cloud

In a public cloud, services are owned and operated by third-party providers like Microsoft Azure. The infrastructure is shared across multiple organizations, offering scalability and cost-effectiveness.

Private Cloud

A private cloud is dedicated to a single organization, providing greater control over resources and security. It can be hosted on-premises or by a third-party provider.

Hybrid Cloud

Hybrid cloud combines public and private clouds, allowing data and applications to be shared between them. This model offers flexibility, optimized infrastructure, and better security.

Cloud Service Models

Infrastructure as a Service (IaaS)

IaaS provides virtualized computing resources over the internet. It’s the most basic cloud service, allowing businesses to rent servers, storage, and networking on a pay-as-you-go basis.

Platform as a Service (PaaS)

PaaS offers a platform for developing, testing, and deploying applications. It removes the need to manage underlying infrastructure, enabling developers to focus on coding and innovation.

Software as a Service (SaaS)

SaaS delivers software applications over the internet, usually on a subscription basis. The cloud provider handles the management of the application, including updates and security.

Serverless Computing

Serverless computing focuses on building applications without worrying about server management. The cloud provider automatically allocates resources as needed, enabling highly scalable and event-driven applications.

Real-World Uses of Cloud Computing

Cloud computing is deeply integrated into our daily lives and business operations. Here are some common applications:

Cloud-Native Applications: Build and deploy scalable web, mobile, and API applications quickly using cloud-native technologies.

Data Storage and Backup: Securely store and recover data from any location with cloud-based storage solutions.

Streaming Services: Stream high-definition video and audio content to a global audience on any device.

Software Delivery: Distribute the latest software versions to users on demand through SaaS models.

Data Analytics: Analyze large datasets in the cloud using AI and machine learning tools to gain valuable insights.

Getting Started with Microsoft Azure

Microsoft Azure is a leading cloud platform offering a comprehensive range of services for businesses of all sizes. Azure supports all cloud models (public, private, and hybrid) and provides extensive IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS offerings. With Azure, you can easily deploy and manage applications, leverage advanced analytics, and integrate AI into your business processes.

Azure stands out with its strong global presence, robust security measures, and seamless integration with other Microsoft products. Whether you’re a startup or a large enterprise, Azure provides the tools you need to build, deploy, and manage applications efficiently.

Learn More About Azure and Cloud:

  • Cloud Computing: Click Here
  • Cloud service types: Click Here
  • Benefits of using cloud services: Click Here
  • Microsoft Azure Fundementals: Click Here

Ready to explore cloud computing? Get started with a free Azure account or try Azure for Students with $100 Azure credits.

Conclusion

Cloud computing is transforming the way businesses operate, offering unparalleled flexibility, scalability, and cost savings. Whether you’re looking to deploy new applications, store and analyze data, or enhance your business’s performance, the cloud provides a powerful foundation to achieve your goals. With platforms like Microsoft Azure, you can harness the full potential of the cloud to drive innovation and growth.

item.title: APIs 101: Introduction to Postman
item.dc:creator: Linuka Arambawela
item.content:encoded:
Unveiling the Secret Ingredient of Modern Apps

Have you ever wondered how different apps on your phone or computer work together seamlessly? The magic behind this collaboration lies in APIs (Application Programming Interfaces). APIs act as messengers, allowing applications to talk to each other and share data effortlessly.
Think of an API as a waiter in a restaurant. You (the user) give your order (request) to the waiter (API), who then tells the kitchen (server) what you want. The kitchen prepares your food (data) and the waiter delivers it back to you.

Why APIs Matter

APIs are crucial because they:
1.Enable Integration: They allow different software systems to work together.
2.Enhance Functionality: Developers can leverage existing functionalities without building from scratch.
3.Drive Efficiency:Automate processes and reduce redundancy.

How APIs Work

APIs operate through requests and responses. A client application sends a request to the server, specifying the desired resource or action. The server processes the request, retrieves or manipulates the necessary data, and sends back a response, typically in JSON or XML format. This interaction is facilitated by HTTP methods like GET, POST, PUT, and DELETE, enabling seamless data exchange and functionality integration.

Types of APIs

1. REST (Representational State Transfer): Uses standard HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE). It’s stateless, scalable, and easy to use.
2. SOAP (Simple Object Access Protocol): Protocol-based and uses XML. It’s highly secure and reliable.
3. GraphQL: Allows clients to request exactly what they need. It’s flexible and efficient.

Building Your First API: A Step-by-Step Guide

To start building an API:
1. Choose Your API Type: Decide between REST, SOAP, or GraphQL.
2. Design Endpoints: Define the routes (URLs) your API will respond to.
3. Set Up a Server: Use frameworks like Express.js (Node.js) or Flask (Python).
4. Implement CRUD Operations: Create, Read, Update, Delete.
5. Test Test Test: Use tools like Postman to ensure your API works as expected.

Best Practices for API Development

1. Documentation: Clearly document your API for users.
2. Versioning: Manage changes without disrupting users.
3. Security: Implement authentication and encryption.
4. Rate Limiting: Prevent abuse by limiting the number of requests.

Postman: Your API Development Powerhouse

Postman is a powerful tool for developing and testing APIs. It provides a user-friendly interface to make HTTP requests, inspect responses, and automate testing. With features like collections, environments, and mock servers, Postman simplifies the API development workflow, making it easier to design, debug, and document APIs.

Real-World Applications

APIs are everywhere. They power social media integrations, payment gateways, weather data, and much more. For instance, when you log in to a website using your Google account, you’re using an API.

Unlocking the Power of AI with APIs

The world of AI is no exception to the power of APIs. Here’s how GPT-3 and Gemini APIs are making waves:

  • GPT-3 APIs: OpenAI offers GPT-3 APIs, allowing developers to integrate powerful text-generation and processing capabilities into their applications. This can be used for tasks like creating marketing copy, generating different creative text formats, or building chatbots with advanced conversational abilities.
  • Gemini APIs: Google’s Gemini APIs provide access to their latest AI models, including multimodal capabilities. This means developers can create applications that work with text, audio, video, and code, opening doors for innovative solutions like generating video descriptions or creating interactive AI experiences.

The Final Takeaway

APIs are a critical skill for any developer. They unlock the power of integration, enhance functionality, and streamline processes. From building your first API to mastering advanced concepts, the journey is challenging but rewarding. Dive in, experiment, and explore the endless possibilities that APIs offer.

Want to Deep Dive? For more detailed information and examples, you can explore the postman documentations [here].

Postman Student Expert

item.title: Beyond HTTP: Leveraging WebSockets for Real-Time Communication
item.dc:creator: Linuka Arambawela
item.content:encoded:
WebSockets are a powerful technology that enables real-time, two-way communication between a web browser and a server. Unlike traditional HTTP requests, which are one-way interactions, WebSockets establish a persistent connection, allowing for continuous data exchange without the need for constant page refreshes. This opens doors for a new level of interactivity and responsiveness in web applications.

Key Features of WebSockets:

  • Two-way communication: Data can flow in both directions simultaneously, between the browser and the server. This eliminates the limitations of HTTP’s request-response cycle.
  • Full-duplex communication: Both parties can send and receive messages at the same time, enabling real-time interactions like chat applications or collaborative editing tools.
  • Persistent connection: WebSockets establish a long-lived connection that remains open until it’s explicitly closed. This eliminates the overhead of repeatedly opening and closing new connections for each data exchange.
  • Lower latency: The persistent connection and efficient data transfer protocol of WebSockets result in lower latency compared to HTTP, making applications feel more responsive.

Advantages of WebSockets:

  • Real-time updates: WebSockets enable applications to display updates and changes instantly, without requiring users to refresh the page. This is ideal for live dashboards, stock tickers, chat applications, and more.
  • Reduced server load: By eliminating the need for frequent HTTP requests, WebSockets can significantly reduce server load, especially for applications that require frequent data updates.
  • Improved user experience: The real-time nature of WebSockets leads to a more seamless and responsive user experience, where data feels constantly in sync.

Disadvantages of WebSockets:

  • Increased complexity: Implementing WebSockets requires additional code and considerations compared to traditional HTTP requests.
  • Browser compatibility: While widely supported, some older browsers may not have full WebSocket functionality.
  • Security concerns: As WebSockets establish a long-lived connection, proper security measures are crucial to prevent unauthorized access or data breaches.

How WebSockets Work:

The process of establishing a WebSocket connection involves a handshake between the browser and the server. Here’s a simplified breakdown:

  1. Handshake initiation: The browser initiates the connection by sending an HTTP request containing specific headers that indicate WebSocket support.
  2. Server response: The server verifies the request and, if successful, sends a response with its own WebSocket headers, establishing the connection.
  3. Data exchange: Once the connection is established, data can be exchanged in both directions using a lightweight messaging protocol. Messages are typically sent in text format (JSON) for easier processing by both parties.
  4. Connection closure: The connection can be closed by either the browser or the server by sending a closing message.

WebSockets vs. HTTP:

Where WebSockets are Used:

WebSockets have found applications in various web development scenarios where real-time communication is crucial. Here are some common use cases:

  • Chat applications: WebSockets enable instant messaging and chat functionality, allowing users to receive and send messages in real-time.
  • Real-time dashboards: Applications displaying live data updates, such as stock tickers or sports scores, benefit significantly from WebSockets.
  • Collaborative editing tools: Documents or files can be edited simultaneously by multiple users, with changes reflected instantly for all participants.
  • Multiplayer games: Real-time updates in online games, like player positions or actions, are facilitated by WebSockets.
  • Social media feeds: Live updates on social media platforms, such as new posts or notifications, can be delivered through WebSockets.

By understanding the capabilities and limitations of WebSockets, web developers can create more engaging and interactive web experiences that leverage the power of real-time communication.

I’ve harnessed the power of WebSockets to create a live chat application! You can experience it in action from here. The source code is available on GitHub for your reference and potential contributions: Github Repo
